description: Ful medames is Egypt's national dish — a humble but deeply satisfying bowl of slow-cooked broad beans (broad beans) dressed with olive oil, lemon juice, garlic, and cumin. Eaten for breakfast by millions of Egyptians daily, it is the ultimate comfort food. The beans are traditionally cooked overnight in a special pot called an idra, resulting in an incredibly creamy, rich consistency. Served with fresh vegetables, pickles, and warm Egyptian baladi bread.

steps.instruction: Drain the soaked beans. Place in a pot with fresh water to cover by 5cm. Bring to a boil, reduce to a very gentle simmer, and cook for 2-3 hours until very soft and creamy. Add water as needed.
Drain most of the liquid, leaving the beans quite moist. Mash some of the beans roughly with the back of a spoon, leaving some whole for texture.
Stir in the garlic, cumin, and salt. Transfer to a serving bowl.
Drizzle generously with olive oil and lemon juice. Sprinkle with cumin, parsley, and chilli if using. Serve warm with pitta bread, sliced tomatoes, and pickles.

image_placeholder: A bowl of creamy ful medames drizzled with olive oil, lemon juice, and cumin, served with warm bread, tomatoes, and pickles.
tips: For the tinned shortcut, simply warm the beans and proceed from step 2 — ready in 10 minutes.
The generous olive oil and lemon juice at the end are essential — do not skimp.
Ful medames is traditionally breakfast food but makes an excellent, inexpensive meal at any time.

title: Halva
description: Halva is the Middle East's quintessential confection — a dense, crumbly-smooth sweet made from tahini and hot sugar syrup. The two elements are combined and worked quickly until the mixture sets into a firm but flaky confection that melts on the tongue. Flavoured with vanilla and sometimes studded with pistachios, chocolate, or dried fruit, halva is served sliced at the end of a meal or as a snack with tea. Making halva at home is surprisingly simple but requires fast, confident handling.

steps.instruction: Line a small loaf tin or container with baking parchment. Warm the tahini in a bowl set over simmering water until pourable and about 50°C. Stir in vanilla.

Make the sugar syrup: combine sugar and water in a saucepan. Heat over medium heat, stirring until dissolved. Boil without stirring until the syrup reaches 120°C (firm ball stage) on a sugar thermometer.

Immediately pour the hot syrup into the warm tahini. Stir vigorously with a wooden spoon until the mixture thickens and begins to pull away from the sides of the bowl, about 1-2 minutes. Fold in pistachios if using.
Quickly press the mixture into the prepared tin. Smooth the top. Allow to cool completely at room temperature, then refrigerate for at least 2 hours until set.
Turn out and slice. Store in an airtight container at room temperature for up to 2 weeks.

image_placeholder: Sliced halva with visible pistachio pieces on a plate, showing the distinctive flaky, crumbly texture.
wine_pairing_notes: Strong Turkish or Arabic coffee, or mint tea.
tips: A sugar thermometer is essential — the syrup must reach exactly 120°C (firm ball stage).
Work quickly once the syrup is added to the tahini — it sets fast.
The tahini must be warm (not cold) when the syrup is added, or the halva will be grainy.

title: Tonkotsu Ramen
description: Tonkotsu ramen is Fukuoka's gift to the world — a bowl of noodles swimming in a milky-white, intensely rich pork bone broth that has been boiled vigorously for 8-12 hours until the collagen and fat emulsify into a creamy, velvety soup. Topped with chashu (braised pork belly), soft-boiled ramen eggs, nori, and spring onions, it is one of Japan's most beloved comfort foods. The broth's opacity comes from sustained, rolling boil — not a gentle simmer — which is the key technique that sets tonkotsu apart.

ingredients.name: pork bones (trotters or neck bones)
ingredients.notes: ask butcher to split large bones

steps.instruction: Blanch the pork bones in boiling water for 10 minutes. Drain, rinse thoroughly, and scrub clean. Place in a large stockpot with 3 litres of fresh water, onion, spring onion whites, ginger, and garlic.
Bring to a vigorous, rolling boil. Maintain this strong boil (NOT a simmer) for 8-12 hours, adding water to maintain the level. Stir occasionally. The broth should turn creamy white.

Meanwhile, make the chashu: roll pork belly tightly and tie with kitchen string. Sear all sides in a hot pan. Braise in a mixture of 200ml soy sauce, mirin, sake, 200ml water, and 2 smashed garlic cloves. Simmer covered for 2 hours until very tender. Cool in the liquid.
Make ramen eggs: soft-boil eggs for 7 minutes. Plunge into ice water. Peel and marinate in leftover chashu braising liquid for at least 4 hours.
Strain the broth through a fine sieve. Season with 2-3 tbsp soy sauce and salt to taste. The broth should be rich, creamy, and intensely porky.

Cook ramen noodles according to package. Place tare (2 tbsp soy sauce) in each bowl. Ladle hot broth over. Add noodles, then top with sliced chashu, halved ramen egg, nori, sliced spring onions, and sesame seeds.

image_placeholder: A bowl of tonkotsu ramen with milky white broth, sliced chashu pork, a halved soft-boiled egg with jammy yolk, nori, and spring onions.
wine_pairing_notes: A cold Japanese lager like Asahi or Sapporo, or a junmai sake.
tips: The key to the creamy white broth is a vigorous, rolling boil — never reduce to a simmer.
This is a weekend project — the broth alone takes 8-12 hours.
Marinating the ramen eggs overnight in chashu liquid gives the best flavour and colour.

title: Chicken Katsu Curry
description: Chicken katsu curry is one of Japan's most popular comfort foods — a crispy, golden panko-crumbed chicken cutlet served over rice with a rich, mildly sweet curry sauce. Japanese curry is distinctly different from Indian or Thai curries: it is thick, mild, and slightly sweet with warm spices. While many Japanese home cooks use curry roux blocks, this recipe makes the sauce from scratch for a more nuanced flavour.

steps.instruction: Make the curry sauce: heat a little oil in a saucepan. Cook onion and carrot for 10 minutes until soft. Add garlic and ginger, cook 1 minute. Add curry powder and flour, stir 2 minutes. Gradually add stock, stirring to avoid lumps. Add soy sauce, honey, and garam masala. Simmer 20 minutes until thick. Blend until smooth if desired.
Prepare the chicken: butterfly each breast to an even 1cm thickness (or pound with a rolling pin between cling film). Season with salt.
Set up a breading station: flour, beaten egg, panko. Dredge each chicken piece in flour, dip in egg, then press firmly into panko to coat evenly.
Heat oil to 170°C. Deep-fry the chicken for 5-6 minutes, turning once, until deep golden and cooked through (75°C internal). Drain on a wire rack. Rest 3 minutes, then slice into strips.
Serve rice on one side of a plate, sliced katsu on the other. Ladle curry sauce alongside. Optionally garnish with pickled red ginger.

image_placeholder: Crispy golden chicken katsu sliced into strips alongside steamed rice, with thick Japanese curry sauce ladled on top.
wine_pairing_notes: A cold Japanese beer (Asahi, Kirin) or a fruity off-dry Riesling.
tips: Press the panko firmly onto the chicken for the crispiest coating.
Fry at 170°C — too hot and the crumb burns before the chicken cooks through.
Japanese curry should be thick enough to not run into the rice — reduce longer if needed.

title: Gyoza
description: Gyoza are Japan's beloved pan-fried dumplings — thin wrappers encasing a juicy filling of minced pork, cabbage, garlic, and ginger. The signature cooking technique involves pan-frying the dumplings to create a crispy golden base, then adding water and covering to steam the tops. The result is the perfect contrast: a crunchy, caramelised bottom and a tender, steamed top. Served with a dipping sauce of soy sauce, rice vinegar, and chilli oil, gyoza are the ultimate snack or starter.

ingredients.name: green cabbage
ingredients.notes: finely chopped, salted and squeezed dry

steps.instruction: Finely chop the cabbage, sprinkle with salt, let sit 10 minutes, then squeeze out all liquid. Mix the pork, cabbage, garlic, ginger, spring onion, soy sauce, and sesame oil. Knead until sticky and well-combined.
Place a heaped teaspoon of filling in the centre of each wrapper. Wet the edges with water. Fold in half and pleat one side to seal, creating 3-4 pleats.
Heat oil in a large frying pan over medium-high heat. Arrange gyoza flat-side down in a single layer. Fry for 2-3 minutes until the bottoms are golden and crispy.
Add the water to the pan and immediately cover with a lid. Steam for 4-5 minutes until wrappers are translucent and filling is cooked through.
Remove lid and cook until remaining water evaporates and the bottoms crisp up again. Serve immediately with a dipping sauce of soy sauce, rice vinegar, and chilli oil.

image_placeholder: A plate of golden-bottomed gyoza arranged in a row, showing the crispy pan-fried base and pleated tops, with a small dish of dipping sauce.
wine_pairing_notes: A crisp Japanese beer or a dry Grüner Veltliner.
tips: Squeeze all the liquid from the salted cabbage — wet filling = soggy gyoza.
The fry-steam-fry technique is the key: fry first for colour, steam to cook, then let the water evaporate to re-crisp.
Gyoza freeze beautifully before cooking — freeze on a tray, then bag. Cook from frozen, adding 1 minute to the steam time.

title: Okonomiyaki
description: Okonomiyaki — literally 'grilled as you like it' — is Osaka's beloved savoury cabbage pancake. A light batter of flour and dashi is loaded with shredded cabbage, then cooked on a griddle with slices of pork belly on top. The pancake is flipped to crisp the pork, then lavished with okonomiyaki sauce, Kewpie mayonnaise, bonito flakes, and aonori (seaweed powder). Watching the bonito flakes dance on top from the residual heat is one of Japan's most mesmerising food experiences.

ingredients.name: okonomiyaki sauce
ingredients.notes: or mix: 2 tbsp ketchup, 1 tbsp Worcestershire sauce, 1 tbsp oyster sauce, 1 tsp honey

steps.instruction: Mix flour and dashi until just combined (do not overmix). Let batter rest 30 minutes in the fridge.
Add shredded cabbage, spring onion, tenkasu, pickled ginger, and 1 egg to half the batter. Mix until cabbage is just coated.
Heat oil in a frying pan over medium heat. Pour the cabbage mixture into the centre of the pan and shape into a thick round pancake about 2cm thick. Lay pork belly slices over the top.
Cook for 5-6 minutes until the bottom is golden brown. Carefully flip the pancake (pork side down now). Cover with a lid and cook for 5 minutes on medium-low until cooked through.

Flip once more so the pork is on top. Brush generously with okonomiyaki sauce, drizzle with Kewpie mayonnaise, and scatter bonito flakes and aonori over the top. Repeat with remaining batter for a second pancake. Serve immediately.

image_placeholder: A golden okonomiyaki topped with dark sauce, white Kewpie mayo zigzags, dancing bonito flakes, and green aonori powder.
wine_pairing_notes: A cold Japanese beer (Asahi Super Dry) or a highball (whisky and soda).
tips: Do not overmix the batter — a few lumps are fine and keep it light.
Cook on medium to medium-low heat — okonomiyaki needs time for the thick centre to cook through.
Cover with a lid while cooking to ensure the cabbage steams and becomes sweet and tender.

title: Miso Shiru
title_en: Miso Soup with Tofu and Wakame
description: Miso soup is served at virtually every Japanese meal — a simple, soothing broth of dashi stock enriched with dissolved miso paste, typically containing cubes of soft tofu and rehydrated wakame seaweed. The key is never boiling the soup after adding the miso, which would kill the beneficial probiotics and produce a harsh flavour. This staple takes minutes to prepare and is one of the most nourishing soups in the world.

ingredients.name: dashi stock
ingredients.notes: made from kombu and bonito flakes, or instant dashi

steps.instruction: Soak the dried wakame in cold water for 5 minutes. Drain and set aside.
Bring the dashi stock to a gentle simmer in a saucepan. Add the tofu cubes and wakame. Simmer gently for 2 minutes.
Remove from heat. Place the miso paste in a ladle, submerge partially in the broth, and stir to dissolve gradually. Never boil the soup after adding miso.
Ladle into bowls and garnish with sliced spring onion. Serve immediately.

image_placeholder: A lacquerware bowl of clear miso soup with cubes of soft white tofu, green wakame seaweed, and sliced spring onion.
tips: Never boil miso soup after adding the miso — this destroys the flavour and probiotics.
Use good quality dashi for the best flavour — it is the foundation of the soup.
White (shiro) miso is milder and sweeter; red (aka) miso is stronger and saltier.

title: Tamagoyaki
description: Tamagoyaki is the beloved Japanese rolled omelette — layers of thin egg are cooked one at a time and rolled upon themselves in a rectangular pan to create a beautiful spiral pattern when sliced. The egg is seasoned with dashi, mirin, and a touch of sugar, giving it a subtly sweet, savoury character. A staple of bento boxes, sushi bars, and Japanese breakfasts, tamagoyaki requires practice but is deeply satisfying once mastered.

steps.instruction: Beat the eggs lightly with dashi, mirin, sugar, soy sauce, and salt. Do not over-whisk — you do not want bubbles.
Heat a rectangular tamagoyaki pan (or a small non-stick pan) over medium-low heat. Oil lightly with a paper towel.
Pour a thin layer of egg mixture (about one-quarter) into the pan. When it is mostly set but still slightly wet on top, roll it towards you using chopsticks or a spatula.

Push the roll to the far end of the pan. Oil the pan again. Pour another thin layer of egg. Lift the rolled egg slightly to let new egg flow underneath. When set, roll over the first roll towards you again. Repeat with remaining egg.
Shape using a bamboo mat or cling film. Let cool slightly, then slice into pieces and serve.

image_placeholder: Sliced tamagoyaki showing the distinctive spiral layers of golden egg, arranged neatly on a small Japanese plate.
tips: Use low to medium-low heat — rushing will brown the egg instead of keeping it golden.
A rectangular tamagoyaki pan makes this much easier, but a small round pan works too.
Roll while the top layer is still slightly wet — it will bond to the next layer as you roll.

title: Onigiri
description: Onigiri are Japan's most popular snack — triangular or oval-shaped rice balls filled with various ingredients like salted salmon, pickled plum (umeboshi), or tuna mayonnaise, wrapped partially in a strip of nori seaweed. Found in every convenience store in Japan, they are the perfect on-the-go food. The rice should be warm when shaping (cooled slightly so you can handle it), seasoned with salt, and pressed firmly but not too tightly.

substitutions.substitute: tinned tuna mixed with Kewpie mayo

steps.instruction: Cook rice according to package instructions. Let cool slightly until you can handle it, but it should still be warm.
Wet your hands with salted water (dissolve salt in a bowl of water). Take about 100g of rice (a generous handful) and flatten slightly in your palm.

Place a tablespoon of flaked salmon in the centre. Fold the rice around the filling and press gently into a triangular shape, cupping your hand to form the corners. Press firmly enough to hold together but not so hard that the rice becomes dense.
Wrap a strip of nori around the base of the triangle. Repeat with remaining rice and filling.
Serve immediately or wrap in cling film for a packed lunch. Best eaten within a few hours.

image_placeholder: Three triangular onigiri with nori seaweed strips, one partially broken to show the salmon filling inside, on a Japanese plate.
tips: Wet your hands with salted water before each onigiri to prevent sticking and season the outside.
Shape the rice while warm — cold rice is difficult to mould and won't stick together as well.
Keep nori separate until just before eating if you want it crispy, or wrap in advance for softer nori.

title: Zaru Soba
title_en: Cold Soba Noodles with Dipping Sauce
description: Zaru soba is a refreshing Japanese dish of chilled buckwheat noodles served on a bamboo mat (zaru) with a concentrated dipping sauce (tsuyu) made from dashi, soy sauce, and mirin. The noodles are cooked, shocked in ice water until perfectly cold and firm, then served with wasabi, grated ginger, and sliced spring onions as condiments. A light, elegant summer dish that showcases the nutty flavour and pleasant bite of quality soba noodles.

steps.instruction: Make the tsuyu: combine dashi, soy sauce, and mirin in a small saucepan. Bring to a brief boil, then cool completely. Chill in the fridge.

Cook soba noodles in a large pot of boiling unsalted water for 4-5 minutes until just tender. Drain and rinse under cold running water, rubbing gently to remove excess starch. Plunge into ice water to chill completely. Drain well.

Arrange the cold soba on a bamboo mat or plate. Serve with individual cups of chilled tsuyu, and small dishes of wasabi, grated ginger, spring onions, and nori strips. Dip the soba into the tsuyu before eating.

image_placeholder: Chilled soba noodles on a bamboo mat with a small cup of dark tsuyu dipping sauce, wasabi, and sliced spring onions.
wine_pairing_notes: A cold junmai sake or a Japanese beer.
tips: Rinse the cooked soba thoroughly under cold water — the starch must be removed for the best texture.
The tsuyu should be concentrated — you dip, not submerge, the noodles.
Soba noodles should be eaten promptly — they become mushy if left sitting too long.

title: Gyudon (Beef Sukiyaki Bowl)
description: This home-style sukiyaki-inspired dish features thinly sliced beef simmered with onions in a sweet soy sauce broth until meltingly tender, served over a bowl of steamed rice. The sweet-savoury sauce — a combination of soy sauce, mirin, sake, and sugar — is the foundation of many Japanese dishes. Topped with a raw or soft-cooked egg that the diner mixes into the hot rice and beef, creating a rich, silky coating.

steps.instruction: Combine dashi, soy sauce, mirin, sake, and sugar in a pan. Bring to a simmer.
Add the sliced onions and cook 5 minutes until softened.
Add the sliced beef and cook 3-4 minutes until just cooked through. Do not overcook.
Divide rice between bowls. Spoon the beef and onions with sauce over the rice. Top each with a raw egg or soft-poached egg. Serve with pickled ginger.

image_placeholder: A donburi bowl of steamed rice topped with tender sliced beef and onions in a glossy sauce, with a raw egg yolk on top.
wine_pairing_notes: A cold Japanese beer or a dry sake.
tips: Slice the beef as thin as possible — partially freezing it for 30 minutes makes this easier.
Do not overcook the beef — it should be tender, not tough.
The raw egg is traditional — use the freshest eggs from a trusted source, or soft-poach them.

title: Chirashi-zushi
description: Chirashi-zushi — scattered sushi — is the easiest way to enjoy sushi at home. A bed of seasoned sushi rice is artfully topped with an array of sashimi-grade raw fish, vegetables, and garnishes. Unlike nigiri or maki, there is no intricate shaping required. The beauty lies in the variety and visual presentation. Common toppings include salmon, tuna, prawns, tamagoyaki (rolled omelette), cucumber, and ikura (salmon roe).

steps.instruction: Cook the rice. While hot, fold in the sushi vinegar (rice vinegar mixed with sugar and salt). Fan the rice to cool it to room temperature while mixing.
Divide the seasoned sushi rice between serving bowls.
Artfully arrange the sliced fish, prawns, and cucumber over the rice. Sprinkle with sesame seeds and nori strips.
Serve with pickled ginger, wasabi, and soy sauce on the side.

image_placeholder: A beautiful chirashi bowl with colourful slices of salmon, tuna, and prawns arranged over white sushi rice, garnished with nori strips and sesame seeds.
wine_pairing_notes: A cold junmai ginjo sake or a dry Champagne.
tips: Fan the rice while seasoning to cool it quickly and give it a glossy sheen.
Ensure your fish is sashimi-grade and from a trusted fishmonger.
The presentation is important — take time to arrange toppings artfully.

title: Nasu Dengaku
description: Nasu dengaku is a classic Japanese dish of halved aubergines roasted until melting soft, then topped with a sweet miso glaze and briefly grilled until the glaze bubbles and caramelises. The glaze — a combination of miso, mirin, sugar, and sake — is deeply umami-rich and perfectly complements the silky aubergine flesh. A popular izakaya dish and a staple of Buddhist temple cuisine (shojin ryori), it is simple, elegant, and intensely flavourful.

steps.instruction: Score the cut surface of the aubergines in a crosshatch pattern. Brush with sesame oil. Roast at 200°C cut-side down for 20 minutes until very soft.
Meanwhile, mix miso, mirin, sugar, and sake in a small pan. Warm over low heat, stirring until smooth and slightly thickened.
Turn the aubergines cut-side up. Spread the miso glaze thickly over the surface. Grill under a hot grill for 3-4 minutes until the glaze bubbles and caramelises.
Garnish with sesame seeds and spring onion. Serve hot.

image_placeholder: Halved aubergines with golden, bubbling miso glaze on top, garnished with sesame seeds and spring onion, on a Japanese plate.
wine_pairing_notes: A junmai sake or a light, fruity Pinot Noir.
tips: Score the aubergine deeply for better cooking and sauce absorption.
The miso glaze should be thick enough to stay on the aubergine — reduce if too thin.
White (shiro) miso gives a sweeter, milder glaze; red (aka) miso is bolder.

title: Matcha Panna Cotta
description: This fusion dessert marries Italian panna cotta technique with Japanese matcha green tea, creating a delicate, creamy dessert with a beautiful pale green colour and a distinctive earthy, slightly bitter-sweet flavour. The high-quality matcha provides antioxidants and a complex, grassy character that balances the richness of the cream. Served chilled with a drizzle of sweetened condensed milk or a scattering of azuki beans.

ingredients.name: matcha powder
ingredients.notes: good quality ceremonial or culinary grade

steps.instruction: Soak the gelatine leaves in cold water for 5 minutes until softened.
Sift the matcha into a small bowl. Add 2 tablespoons of warm milk and whisk until smooth with no lumps.

Gently heat the cream, remaining milk, sugar, and vanilla in a saucepan until steaming but not boiling. Remove from heat. Squeeze excess water from the gelatine and stir into the warm cream until fully dissolved.
Whisk in the matcha paste until evenly combined and the colour is uniform pale green. Strain through a fine sieve into a jug.
Pour into 4-6 moulds or glasses. Chill in the fridge for at least 4 hours or overnight until set.

image_placeholder: A pale green matcha panna cotta in a glass, with a smooth, jiggly surface, served on a Japanese plate.
wine_pairing_notes: A glass of cold hojicha tea or a sweet sake.
tips: Sift the matcha thoroughly and dissolve in warm liquid first to avoid lumps.
Use good quality matcha — cheap matcha tastes bitter and chalky.
Do not overheat the cream mixture — just to steaming, not boiling.

title: Yakitori
description: Yakitori are bite-sized pieces of chicken grilled on small bamboo skewers over white-hot charcoal, basted with a sweet-savoury tare sauce. Found at yakitori-ya (specialist grill restaurants) and izakaya across Japan, the best yakitori is characterised by charred edges, juicy meat, and a lacquered, caramelised glaze. Different cuts of chicken are used — thigh meat (momo) is the most popular for its juiciness — and traditionally seasoned simply with either tare (sauce) or shio (salt).

steps.instruction: Make the tare: combine soy sauce, mirin, sake, and sugar in a saucepan. Bring to a boil, then simmer for 10 minutes until reduced and syrupy. Set aside.
Thread alternating pieces of chicken and spring onion onto soaked bamboo skewers. Season lightly with salt.
Grill over very high heat (charcoal is ideal) for 2-3 minutes per side. During the last minute, brush with tare sauce and turn to caramelise. Brush again and serve immediately.
Serve with a sprinkle of shichimi togarashi if desired.

image_placeholder: Glossy yakitori skewers with charred edges on a rectangular plate, brushed with tare sauce, with a small dish of shichimi togarashi.
wine_pairing_notes: A cold Japanese beer, highball whisky soda, or a dry junmai sake.
tips: Soak bamboo skewers for at least 30 minutes to prevent burning.
Apply the tare only in the last minute of grilling — too early and the sugar burns.
High, direct heat is essential — a charcoal grill gives the best results.

title: Oyakodon
description: Oyakodon — literally 'parent and child bowl' — features tender chicken and soft-set scrambled egg simmered together in a sweet dashi-soy broth, served over steamed rice. This beloved donburi (rice bowl dish) is the epitome of Japanese comfort food: quick, simple, nourishing, and deeply satisfying. The egg should be just barely set, creating a custardy, silky sauce that coats the rice. It is one of the first dishes many Japanese home cooks learn to make.

steps.instruction: In a wide shallow pan, combine dashi, soy sauce, mirin, and sugar. Bring to a simmer. Add the sliced onion and cook 3 minutes until softened.
Add the chicken pieces. Simmer for 5-6 minutes until the chicken is just cooked through.

Pour about two-thirds of the beaten eggs over the chicken and onions. Cover and cook 1 minute. Then add the remaining egg and cover for 30 seconds — the egg should be just barely set, still slightly custardy.
Immediately slide the chicken and egg onto bowls of hot steamed rice. Garnish with spring onion and nori strips. Serve at once.

image_placeholder: A donburi bowl of steamed rice topped with chicken pieces and silky, just-set scrambled egg in a glossy sauce, garnished with spring onion.
wine_pairing_notes: A cold Japanese beer or a warm honjozo sake.
tips: The egg should be barely set — still custardy and slightly runny on top.
Adding the egg in two stages creates a mix of textures: set and custardy.
Use a wide, shallow pan so the egg cooks evenly in a thin layer.

title: Kimchi Jjigae
description: Kimchi jjigae is Korea's most beloved comfort stew — a fiery, deeply flavourful one-pot dish of well-fermented kimchi simmered with pork belly, tofu, and gochugaru. The key is using aged kimchi (at least 2-3 weeks old) whose tanginess intensifies during cooking. The stew simmers quickly in a stone or clay pot, arriving at the table still bubbling. Served with steamed rice, it is the quintessential Korean home-cooked meal.

ingredients.name: aged napa cabbage kimchi
ingredients.notes: at least 2-3 weeks old, with juices

steps.instruction: Heat sesame oil in a clay pot or heavy saucepan over medium heat. Add the pork belly and cook until the fat renders and the pork is lightly browned, about 3-4 minutes.
Add the kimchi (with its juices) and cook for 3-4 minutes, stirring until the kimchi softens.
Add gochugaru, gochujang, soy sauce, garlic, sugar, and water. Bring to a boil, then reduce to a simmer. Cook for 15 minutes until the flavours meld and the pork is tender.
Add the tofu slices and spring onion. Simmer for 5 more minutes. Serve immediately, still bubbling, with steamed rice.

image_placeholder: A bubbling clay pot of kimchi jjigae with pork belly, tofu, and bright red kimchi, served with steamed rice.
wine_pairing_notes: A cold Korean beer (Cass, Hite) or soju.
tips: The kimchi must be well-fermented (sour) — fresh kimchi lacks the depth needed.
Do not add salt until the end — kimchi and soy sauce provide plenty.
A clay or stone pot retains heat and keeps the stew bubbling at the table.

title: Bibimbap
description: Bibimbap — meaning 'mixed rice' — is Korea's most internationally famous dish. A bowl of steamed rice is topped with an array of individually seasoned vegetables (namul), a protein (often beef bulgogi or a fried egg), and a generous spoonful of gochujang sauce. The diner mixes everything together vigorously before eating, creating a harmonious blend of flavours and textures. Jeonju in South Korea is considered the birthplace of the finest bibimbap.

ingredients.name: spinach
ingredients.notes: blanched, seasoned with sesame oil and soy sauce

ingredients.name: minced beef
ingredients.notes: seasoned with soy sauce, sesame oil, garlic

substitutions.substitute: mix of miso and chilli flakes

steps.instruction: Prepare each vegetable separately: blanch spinach and beansprouts, season with sesame oil and soy sauce. Sauté julienned carrot, courgette, and mushrooms separately with a drop of sesame oil and pinch of salt.
Season the beef with soy sauce, sesame oil, garlic, and pepper. Sauté quickly over high heat until browned.
Fry eggs sunny-side up with runny yolks.
Divide rice between bowls. Arrange each vegetable, the beef, and egg on top in separate sections. Add a generous spoonful of gochujang and a drizzle of sesame oil. Sprinkle with sesame seeds.
Mix everything together vigorously before eating. The more you mix, the better it tastes.

image_placeholder: A colourful bibimbap bowl with rice, arranged vegetables, beef, and a fried egg on top with a dollop of red gochujang.
wine_pairing_notes: A cold Korean beer or a dry Riesling.
tips: Season each vegetable separately — this is what gives bibimbap its complexity.
The more vigorously you mix, the better the flavours meld.
For dolsot (stone pot) bibimbap, heat an oiled stone bowl until smoking hot for crispy rice.

title: Japchae
description: Japchae is a beloved Korean dish of sweet potato glass noodles stir-fried with vegetables and beef in a sweet soy-sesame sauce. Originally a royal court dish, it is now served at celebrations and family gatherings. The translucent, chewy noodles are tossed with individually sautéed vegetables for the best flavour.

substitutions.notes: ensure they are sweet potato starch-based

steps.instruction: Cook glass noodles in boiling water for 6-7 minutes until tender. Drain and cut into shorter lengths. Toss with 1 tbsp soy sauce, 1 tbsp sugar, and 1 tbsp sesame oil.
Sauté each vegetable separately with a drop of oil and pinch of salt. Sauté the beef with soy sauce and garlic.
Combine noodles, vegetables, beef, and remaining seasoning. Toss well. Sprinkle with sesame seeds. Serve warm or at room temperature.

image_placeholder: A plate of glossy japchae noodles with colourful julienned vegetables and sesame seeds.
wine_pairing_notes: A dry rosé or a cold Korean beer.
tips: Sauté each vegetable separately for the best flavour and texture.
Cut the cooked noodles with scissors — they can be very long.
Japchae tastes great at room temperature, making it ideal for parties.

title: Tteokbokki
description: Tteokbokki is Korea's most popular street food — chewy cylindrical rice cakes simmered in a fiery, sweet-spicy gochujang sauce. Found at every street food stall in Seoul, the dish is addictively sweet, spicy, and savoury. Fish cakes and boiled eggs are common additions.

steps.instruction: Bring the stock to a boil. Add gochujang, gochugaru, sugar, and soy sauce. Stir until dissolved.
Add the rice cakes and fish cakes. Simmer for 8-10 minutes, stirring frequently, until the rice cakes are soft and the sauce is thick and glossy.
Add spring onions. Cook 1 more minute. Serve immediately, sprinkled with sesame seeds.

image_placeholder: A bowl of glossy tteokbokki in a vibrant red sauce with fish cakes and spring onions.
wine_pairing_notes: A cold Korean beer or soju.
tips: Stir frequently as the sauce thickens — rice cakes can stick.
Soak frozen rice cakes in warm water for 20 minutes before cooking.
The sauce should be thick enough to coat the rice cakes — not watery.

title: Yangnyeom Chicken
description: Yangnyeom chicken is Korea's iconic fried chicken — double-fried for an ultra-crispy, shattering coating, then tossed in a sticky, sweet-spicy gochujang sauce. The double-frying technique is what makes Korean fried chicken uniquely crispy without being heavy. The sauce balances gochujang heat with ketchup sweetness, garlic, and rice vinegar.

steps.instruction: Season chicken with salt, pepper, and ginger. Coat thoroughly in cornflour.
First fry: heat oil to 160°C. Fry chicken in batches for 8-10 minutes until cooked through. Remove and rest on a wire rack for 5 minutes.
Second fry: increase oil to 190°C. Fry again for 3-4 minutes until very golden and crispy. Drain.
Make sauce: combine gochujang, ketchup, honey, soy sauce, rice vinegar, and garlic in a saucepan. Simmer 2 minutes until glossy.
Toss the hot chicken in the sauce. Sprinkle with sesame seeds and sliced spring onion. Serve immediately.

image_placeholder: Glossy, red yangnyeom chicken wings on a plate, glistening with sauce and sprinkled with sesame seeds and spring onion.
tips: Double-frying is the secret — the first fry cooks, the second fry crisps.
Use cornflour (not wheat flour) for the lightest, crispiest coating.
Toss in the sauce immediately before serving — the coating softens over time.

title: Sundubu-jjigae
description: Sundubu-jjigae is a bubbling Korean stew starring silky soft (uncurdled) tofu in a spicy seafood or pork broth. The stew is fiery red from gochugaru and deeply savoury from anchovy stock or seafood. A raw egg cracked into the bubbling stew at the table sets from the residual heat, adding richness.

substitutions.notes: different brand may vary in softness

steps.instruction: Heat sesame oil in a clay pot. Sauté pork belly 3 minutes. Add garlic and gochugaru, cook 1 minute.
Add stock, soy sauce, and seafood if using. Bring to a boil and simmer 5 minutes.
Gently add the silken tofu in large spoonfuls (do not break up too much). Simmer 5 minutes.
Crack a raw egg into the bubbling stew. Add spring onion. Serve immediately, still bubbling, with rice.

image_placeholder: A bubbling clay pot of sundubu-jjigae with silky tofu in a red broth, with an egg setting on top.
tips: Use the softest tofu available — it should barely hold its shape.
A clay pot keeps the stew bubbling at the table.
The egg should be cracked in at the very end — it sets from residual heat.

title: Kimchi Bokkeumbap
description: Kimchi fried rice is Korea's favourite way to use up leftover rice and aged kimchi. The kimchi is stir-fried until slightly caramelised, then tossed with day-old rice, sesame oil, and often a bit of gochujang for extra punch. Topped with a fried egg with a runny yolk that acts as a rich sauce when broken.

substitutions.notes: short-grain is more authentic but any leftover rice works

steps.instruction: Heat vegetable oil in a large wok or frying pan over high heat. Stir-fry the chopped kimchi for 3-4 minutes until slightly caramelised.
Add the cold rice, breaking up any clumps. Stir-fry for 3-4 minutes. Add soy sauce, gochujang if using, and sugar. Toss well.
Drizzle with sesame oil and spring onion. Fry eggs sunny-side up. Serve the rice topped with the fried egg and sesame seeds.

image_placeholder: A plate of kimchi fried rice topped with a sunny-side-up egg with runny yolk, sesame seeds, and spring onion.
tips: Day-old cold rice is essential — fresh rice is too moist and becomes mushy.
Cook on high heat for the best wok hei (smoky flavour).
Break the yolk into the rice while eating for a rich, saucy finish.

title: Gimbap
description: Gimbap is Korea's answer to sushi rolls — seasoned rice and various fillings rolled in nori seaweed and sliced into bite-sized rounds. Unlike Japanese sushi, gimbap rice is seasoned with sesame oil and salt (not vinegar), and fillings are cooked. Common fillings include pickled yellow radish (danmuji), spinach, carrot, egg, and beef. A favourite picnic and packed lunch food.

ingredients.name: eggs
ingredients.notes: made into a thin omelette, cut into strips

steps.instruction: Season warm rice with sesame oil and salt. Prepare all fillings.
Place a nori sheet on a bamboo mat. Spread a thin layer of rice, leaving 2cm at the far edge. Arrange fillings in a line across the centre.
Roll tightly using the mat. Seal the edge with a drop of water. Brush the outside with sesame oil.
Slice into 8-10 rounds with a sharp, wet knife. Serve with pickled ginger.

image_placeholder: Sliced gimbap rolls showing the colourful spiral cross-section of rice, vegetables, and egg, on a plate.
wine_pairing_notes: A cold Korean beer.
tips: Spread rice thinly — too thick and the roll won't close.
Wet the knife between cuts to prevent sticking.
Gimbap is best eaten within a few hours — the nori softens over time.

title: Pajeon
description: Pajeon is a crispy, savoury Korean pancake loaded with spring onions. The batter is thin and the spring onions plentiful, creating a pancake that is more onion than batter. Often enriched with seafood (haemul pajeon), the simpler spring onion version is a beloved snack and side dish, especially on rainy days in Korea where tradition dictates eating pajeon with makgeolli (rice wine).

substitutions.notes: for a different but equally delicious variation

steps.instruction: Mix flour, cornflour, salt, egg, and ice-cold water into a thin batter. Do not overmix — some lumps are fine.
Heat oil in a large frying pan over medium-high heat. Arrange the spring onions in the pan. Pour the batter over them.
Cook for 3-4 minutes until the bottom is golden and crispy. Flip and cook another 3 minutes. Press down with a spatula for maximum crispiness.
Slice into pieces. Serve with dipping sauce (soy sauce, rice vinegar, sesame seeds, and optional chilli).

image_placeholder: A crispy, golden pajeon loaded with spring onions, cut into wedges, with a small dish of soy dipping sauce.
wine_pairing_notes: Korean makgeolli (rice wine) is the traditional pairing.
tips: Use ice-cold water and do not overmix — this keeps the pancake light and crispy.
Press down with a spatula while cooking for extra crispiness.
More spring onion, less batter is the key to great pajeon.

title: Bulgogi
description: Bulgogi — meaning 'fire meat' — is Korea's most famous BBQ dish. Thinly sliced beef is marinated in a sweet, savoury blend of soy sauce, sugar, sesame oil, garlic, ginger, and grated Asian pear (which tenderises the meat), then grilled or pan-fried. The caramelised, smoky edges and tender, flavourful meat make bulgogi irresistible.

ingredients.name: beef sirloin or rib-eye
ingredients.notes: very thinly sliced (freeze briefly for easier slicing)

substitutions.substitute: 1 tbsp honey plus 1 tbsp apple juice

steps.instruction: Combine soy sauce, sugar, sesame oil, grated pear, garlic, ginger, onion, and pepper. Add the sliced beef and marinate for at least 1 hour, ideally overnight.
Cook on a very hot griddle, grill, or frying pan. Cook in a single layer (do not crowd) for 1-2 minutes per side until caramelised.
Garnish with sesame seeds and spring onion. Serve with rice, lettuce leaves for wrapping, and ssamjang (dipping sauce).

image_placeholder: Glistening bulgogi beef with sesame seeds on lettuce leaves with rice and dipping sauce.
wine_pairing_notes: A cold Korean beer or a fruity red wine.
tips: Slice the beef paper-thin — partially freezing for 30 minutes helps.
Grated Asian pear is a natural tenderiser — it makes a significant difference.
Cook on the highest heat possible for the best caramelisation.

title: Galbi
description: Galbi (or kalbi) are Korea's prized BBQ beef short ribs, marinated in a luscious sweet soy sauce with garlic and sesame. The ribs are cut LA-style (across the bone in thin strips) for maximum surface area and quick grilling. The marinade caramelises beautifully over high heat, creating sticky, charred, intensely flavourful meat.

substitutions.notes: ask your butcher for thin cross-cut short ribs

steps.instruction: Soak the ribs in cold water for 30 minutes to draw out blood. Drain and pat dry.
Mix all marinade ingredients. Add the ribs and marinate for at least 4 hours, ideally overnight.
Grill over high heat (charcoal preferred) for 3-4 minutes per side until charred and caramelised. Cut into pieces with scissors.
Serve with steamed rice, lettuce wraps, ssamjang, and kimchi.

image_placeholder: Charred, glistening galbi short ribs on a grill plate with sesame seeds and lettuce wraps.
wine_pairing_notes: A bold Korean soju or a full-bodied red wine.
tips: LA-cut ribs are essential — ask your butcher to cut them thin across the bone.
Overnight marination makes a significant difference.
Use scissors to cut the grilled ribs into bite-sized pieces at the table — traditional Korean style.

title: Doenjang-jjigae
description: Doenjang-jjigae is Korea's hearty soybean paste stew — earthy, deeply savoury, and intensely umami. Made with doenjang (fermented soybean paste), tofu, courgette, and often anchovy stock, it is considered even more essential to the Korean table than kimchi jjigae. The fermented doenjang provides a complex, funky depth of flavour similar to miso but bolder.

steps.instruction: Bring the stock to a boil. Add the doenjang and stir to dissolve.
Add onion, courgette, and garlic. Simmer 10 minutes until vegetables are tender.
Add tofu, chilli, and spring onion. Simmer 5 more minutes. Serve bubbling with steamed rice.

image_placeholder: A bubbling stone pot of doenjang-jjigae with tofu cubes, courgette, and green chilli.
wine_pairing_notes: A cold Korean beer or warm barley tea.
tips: Doenjang is bolder and funkier than miso — that is the intended flavour.
Anchovy stock adds depth, but plain water works for vegetarian.
Do not add salt — doenjang is very salty on its own.

title: Hotteok
description: Hotteok are Korea's beloved winter street food — soft, chewy dough pancakes filled with a mixture of brown sugar, cinnamon, and chopped nuts that melts into a gooey, sweet filling during cooking. The outside is crispy and golden while the inside is a pool of molten sweet syrup. Eaten piping hot on cold winter days from street vendors across Seoul.

substitutions.notes: works if you cannot find glutinous rice flour separately

steps.instruction: Mix flours, yeast, sugar, and salt. Add warm milk. Knead 5 minutes until smooth. Cover and rise 1 hour.
Mix brown sugar, cinnamon, and nuts for the filling.
Divide dough into 8 balls. Flatten each, place a tablespoon of filling in the centre, and seal by pinching edges together.
Heat oil in a frying pan. Place hotteok seam-side down. Cook 2 minutes, then flatten gently with a spatula. Flip and cook 2 more minutes until golden on both sides.

image_placeholder: Golden hotteok on a plate, one broken open to show the gooey brown sugar and nut filling.
wine_pairing_notes: Korean citrus tea (yujacha).
tips: Be careful when biting — the filling is molten hot.
Flatten gently with a spatula for crispy, even cooking.
The glutinous rice flour gives hotteok their distinctive chewiness.

title: Bingsu
description: Bingsu (or patbingsu) is Korea's beloved summer dessert — finely shaved milk ice topped with sweet red beans (pat), tteok (rice cake pieces), fruit, and condensed milk. The ice should be shaved so finely that it resembles fluffy snow, not chunky ice. Modern versions include toppings like mango, matcha, or Oreo, but the classic pat bingsu with red beans remains the most traditional.

steps.instruction: Freeze the milk in a shallow container until solid (at least 6 hours or overnight).
Using a bingsu machine, ice shaver, or food processor, shave the frozen milk into very fine, fluffy flakes. Transfer to chilled bowls immediately.
Top with sweetened red bean paste, rice cake pieces, sliced fruit, and a drizzle of condensed milk. Sprinkle with soybean powder if using. Serve immediately before it melts.

image_placeholder: A tall bowl of fluffy shaved milk ice topped with red beans, rice cakes, fruit, and condensed milk.
wine_pairing_notes: Korean citrus tea or just water.
tips: The milk must be shaved very finely — chunky ice is not bingsu.
Chill the bowls beforehand so the ice melts more slowly.
Serve and eat immediately — bingsu waits for nobody.

title: Mapo Tofu
description: Mapo tofu is Sichuan's most famous dish — soft tofu cubes braised in a fiery, mouth-numbing sauce of doubanjiang (chilli bean paste), Sichuan peppercorns, and minced pork. Created in the 1860s by a pockmarked grandmother (ma po) in Chengdu, the dish exemplifies Sichuan's 'mala' (numbing-spicy) flavour profile. The key ingredients are Pixian doubanjiang and freshly ground Sichuan peppercorns.

steps.instruction: Gently simmer the tofu cubes in salted water for 3 minutes to firm them up. Drain carefully.
Heat oil in a wok. Add Sichuan peppercorns and fry until dark and fragrant. Remove. Add minced pork and cook until browned.
Add doubanjiang and cook, stirring constantly, for 2 minutes until the oil turns red. Add garlic and ginger, cook 30 seconds.
Add stock, Shaoxing wine, and soy sauce. Add the blanched tofu. Simmer gently 8 minutes, shaking the wok (do not stir or the tofu will break).
Add the cornflour slurry and gently shake to thicken. Sprinkle with ground Sichuan peppercorn and spring onion. Serve with steamed rice.

image_placeholder: Cubes of silky tofu in a glistening, red, oily sauce with minced pork and green spring onion, in a bowl.
wine_pairing_notes: A cold Tsingtao beer or a dry Grüner Veltliner.
tips: Use Pixian doubanjiang — it is the soul of this dish.
Shake the wok rather than stirring to avoid breaking the delicate tofu.
Toast and grind fresh Sichuan peppercorns for the best numbing effect.

title: Kung Pao Chicken
description: Kung pao chicken (gong bao ji ding) is a classic Sichuan stir-fry of diced chicken, dried chillies, Sichuan peppercorns, and roasted peanuts in a tangy-sweet sauce. Named after a Qing dynasty governor, the dish balances spicy, sweet, sour, and numbing flavours. The dried chillies provide aromatic heat, and the Chinkiang vinegar adds a distinctive tang.

ingredients.name: cornflour
ingredients.notes: for marinade + 1 tsp mixed with water for sauce

steps.instruction: Marinate the diced chicken with 1 tsp cornflour, 1 tsp soy sauce, and Shaoxing wine for 15 minutes. Mix the sauce: remaining soy sauce, dark soy, vinegar, sugar, and cornflour slurry.

Heat oil in a wok over high heat. Add dried chillies and Sichuan peppercorns, stir-fry 30 seconds until fragrant and darkened (do not burn). Add the chicken and stir-fry 3-4 minutes until just cooked.
Add garlic and ginger, stir-fry 30 seconds. Pour in the sauce mixture, toss to coat. Add peanuts and spring onions. Toss for 30 seconds. Serve with steamed rice.

image_placeholder: Kung pao chicken in a wok with dried chillies, peanuts, and spring onions in a glossy sauce.
wine_pairing_notes: A cold beer or an off-dry Riesling.
tips: Do not burn the dried chillies — they should darken, not blacken.
Have everything prepped before you start — stir-frying moves fast.
Chinkiang vinegar is essential for the authentic flavour — it is different from rice vinegar.

title: Char Siu
description: Char siu is Cantonese barbecued pork with a distinctive lacquered, caramelised glaze of red. Pork shoulder or belly is marinated in a sweet-savoury mixture of hoisin, honey, five-spice, and soy sauce, then roasted at high heat, basting frequently with the marinade. The result is charred, sticky, sweet, and deeply savoury — one of the pillars of Cantonese roast meats.

steps.instruction: Mix all marinade ingredients. Add the pork strips and coat well. Marinate overnight in the fridge (at least 6 hours).
Preheat oven to 220°C. Place pork on a wire rack over a tray lined with foil and water (to catch drips and create steam). Reserve the marinade.
Roast for 15 minutes. Reduce to 180°C, baste with marinade, and roast for another 15 minutes. Baste again and roast 10 more minutes.
Mix remaining marinade with extra honey. Brush over the pork and grill for 3-4 minutes until charred and lacquered. Rest 10 minutes before slicing.

image_placeholder: Glistening char siu sliced into pieces showing the charred edges and pink interior, on a chopping board.
wine_pairing_notes: A Gewürztraminer or an off-dry Riesling.
tips: Overnight marination is essential for the deepest flavour.
Baste frequently for the best lacquered finish.
Rest before slicing to keep the juices in.

title: Xiaolongbao
description: Xiaolongbao are Shanghai's famous soup dumplings — delicate, pleated steamed buns filled with pork and a rich, gelatinous broth that liquefies during steaming to create a burst of hot soup when bitten. The key is making an aspic from pork skin or pig trotter stock that sets when cold and melts when steamed. Eating them requires technique: place on a spoon, nibble a hole, sip the soup, then eat.

ingredients.name: pork aspic (cooled stock jelly)
ingredients.notes: from simmering pork skin with water, diced

substitutions.substitute: rich gelatinous pork stock, chilled until set

steps.instruction: Make the aspic: simmer pork skin or bones with water for 3 hours until very gelatinous. Strain, chill overnight until firmly set. Dice the jelly into small cubes.
Make the dough: combine flour with boiling water, then cold water. Knead 10 minutes until smooth. Rest 30 minutes.
Mix the pork mince with soy sauce, sesame oil, sugar, wine, spring onion, ginger, and diced aspic cubes. Chill.
Roll the dough into a log, cut 24 pieces. Roll each into a thin circle (thinner edges, thicker centre). Place filling in centre, pleat and seal with 18+ folds.
Steam in bamboo steamers lined with baking parchment for 8-10 minutes. Serve immediately with black vinegar and julienned ginger.

image_placeholder: Xiaolongbao in a bamboo steamer, showing their pleated tops, with a dipping dish of black vinegar and ginger.
wine_pairing_notes: A cold Tsingtao beer or a dry Riesling.
tips: The aspic is what creates the soup — do not skip this step.
Eat carefully: place on a spoon, nibble a hole, sip the soup, then eat.
The wrapper should be thin at the edges and slightly thicker at the centre to support the filling.

title: Dan Dan Mian
description: Dan dan noodles are Sichuan's most famous street noodle — thin wheat noodles served in a spicy, nutty sauce of chilli oil, Sichuan peppercorns, preserved mustard greens (ya cai), sesame paste, and minced pork. Named after the carrying pole (dan) used by street vendors, the dish is small in portion but massive in flavour.

steps.instruction: Mix the sauce in each bowl: sesame paste, chilli oil, soy sauce, vinegar, sugar, and Sichuan peppercorn powder.
Brown the minced pork in a dry wok until crispy. Add ya cai and cook 1 minute.
Cook the noodles. Add a ladle of noodle water to each sauce bowl and stir. Drain noodles and add to bowls. Top with crispy pork, ya cai, peanuts, and spring onion.

image_placeholder: A bowl of dan dan noodles with minced pork, chilli oil, peanuts, and spring onion.
wine_pairing_notes: A cold Tsingtao beer.
tips: The sauce is assembled in the bowl — noodle cooking water loosens it.
Ya cai (preserved mustard greens) adds an essential salty-savoury crunch.
Adjust chilli oil to your heat preference.

title: Congee with Century Egg
description: Congee (jook) is Cantonese rice porridge — rice cooked in vast amounts of water until it breaks down into a thick, silky, comforting porridge. Century egg and pork congee (pi dan shou rou zhou) is one of the most popular versions, combining the creamy, funky, alkaline century egg with tender shredded pork. It is the quintessential Cantonese comfort food and breakfast staple.

steps.instruction: Bring rice and water to a boil with ginger slices. Reduce to a low simmer, stirring occasionally, for 1-1.5 hours until the rice has broken down into a thick, creamy porridge.
Season the pork with soy sauce, sesame oil, and white pepper. Stir into the congee and cook 5 minutes.
Add the diced century eggs and cook 2 more minutes. Season with salt.
Serve topped with spring onion, fried shallots, and an extra drizzle of sesame oil.

image_placeholder: A bowl of silky white congee with diced century egg and pork, topped with spring onion.
wine_pairing_notes: Chinese tea (pu-erh or jasmine).
tips: The rice must completely break down — stir regularly and cook for at least 1 hour.
Century eggs have a distinctive funky flavour — start with less if you are new to them.
White pepper is traditional — not black pepper.

title: Cantonese Steamed Fish
title_en: Cantonese Steamed Fish with Ginger and Spring Onion
description: Cantonese steamed fish is the pinnacle of Cantonese cooking philosophy — letting the quality of the ingredient speak for itself. A whole, impeccably fresh fish is steamed until just cooked, then showered with julienned ginger and spring onion and finished with a drizzle of hot oil and seasoned soy sauce. The sizzle of oil hitting the aromatics releases an intoxicating fragrance.

ingredients.name: whole sea bass or sea bream

steps.instruction: Score the fish 3 times on each side. Place sliced ginger inside the cavity and on the plate underneath the fish. Drizzle with Shaoxing wine.
Steam over rapidly boiling water for 8-10 minutes until the fish is just cooked (flesh should be opaque and flake easily).
Carefully drain off any liquid from the plate. Scatter julienned ginger and spring onion over the fish. Mix soy sauce and sugar, pour over.
Heat the vegetable oil in a small pan until smoking. Pour the hot oil over the ginger and spring onion — it will sizzle dramatically. Serve immediately.

image_placeholder: A whole steamed sea bass on a platter with julienned ginger and spring onion, glistening with soy sauce and hot oil.
wine_pairing_notes: A dry white Burgundy or a cold sake.
tips: Freshness of the fish is paramount — this dish has nowhere to hide.
Do not overcook — 8-10 minutes for a 600g fish.
The hot oil sizzle at the end is essential — it releases the ginger and spring onion aromas.

title: Ma La Xiang Guo
description: Ma la xiang guo is Sichuan's popular 'dry pot' — a mix-and-match stir-fry of chosen proteins and vegetables tossed in a fiery, numbing sauce of doubanjiang, dried chillies, and Sichuan peppercorns. Unlike hot pot, this is a dry stir-fry. Diners choose their ingredients (lotus root, tofu skin, prawns, beef, mushrooms) and they are stir-fried together in a wok with an intense chilli-numbing sauce.

ingredients.name: selection of proteins and vegetables
ingredients.notes: e.g. prawns, pork belly, lotus root, wood ear mushrooms, broccoli, tofu skin

steps.instruction: Par-cook harder vegetables and proteins as needed (blanch lotus root, pre-cook prawns etc). Drain well.
Heat oil in a wok over high heat. Add dried chillies and Sichuan peppercorns. Fry 30 seconds. Add doubanjiang and stir-fry 1 minute until oil turns red.
Add garlic and ginger, cook 30 seconds. Add all the proteins and vegetables. Stir-fry on high heat for 3-4 minutes.
Add soy sauce, wine, sugar. Toss well. Finish with sesame oil, spring onion, and sesame seeds.

image_placeholder: A sizzling ma la xiang guo in a wok with an assortment of meats and vegetables glistening with red chilli oil.
wine_pairing_notes: A cold Tsingtao beer.
tips: Par-cook ingredients with different cooking times so everything finishes together.
The wok must be screaming hot for the best wok hei.
Customise the ingredients to your preference — that is the whole point.

title: Cong You Bing
description: Cong you bing (spring onion pancakes) are one of China's most popular street foods — flaky, crispy, chewy flatbreads with layers of fragrant spring onion. The dough is rolled thin, brushed with sesame oil, scattered with spring onions and salt, then rolled up and coiled to create dozens of flaky layers. Pan-fried until golden and crispy on both sides.

steps.instruction: Mix flour with boiling water and salt. Knead 5 minutes until smooth. Cover and rest 30 minutes.

Divide into 4 balls. Roll each into a thin rectangle. Brush with sesame oil, scatter with spring onion and a pinch of salt. Roll up tightly into a log, then coil into a spiral. Flatten gently and roll out again into a 20cm disc.
Pan-fry in oil over medium heat for 3-4 minutes per side until golden and crispy. Cut into wedges and serve hot.

image_placeholder: Golden, flaky spring onion pancakes cut into wedges showing the layers inside.
wine_pairing_notes: Chinese tea or a cold beer.
tips: Boiling water makes the dough softer and more pliable than cold water.
The coil-and-flatten technique creates the flaky layers.
Cook on medium heat — too high and the outside burns before the inside cooks through.

title: Dan Hua Tang
description: Egg drop soup (dan hua tang) is one of China's simplest and most comforting soups — wisps of beaten egg swirled into a hot, well-seasoned chicken broth to create delicate egg ribbons. The technique of slowly drizzling egg into simmering (not boiling) broth while stirring in one direction creates the silky egg threads. Often enriched with tofu and sweetcorn.

steps.instruction: Bring the chicken stock to a simmer. Season with soy sauce, salt, and white pepper.
Stir in the cornflour slurry to slightly thicken the broth.
Reduce to a gentle simmer. While stirring slowly in one direction with a fork, drizzle the beaten egg in a thin stream. The egg should form delicate ribbons.
Remove from heat immediately. Drizzle with sesame oil and garnish with spring onion. Serve at once.

image_placeholder: A bowl of clear golden egg drop soup with delicate egg ribbons and sliced spring onion.
wine_pairing_notes: Chinese tea.
tips: The broth must be simmering, not boiling — boiling makes tough, chunky eggs instead of silky ribbons.
Drizzle the egg in a very thin stream for the most delicate ribbons.
Use homemade or good quality stock — it is the foundation of this simple soup.

title: Gu Lao Rou
title_en: Authentic Cantonese Sweet and Sour Pork
description: Authentic Cantonese sweet and sour pork (gu lao rou) is worlds apart from the takeaway version. Tender pieces of pork are battered and fried until golden and crispy, then tossed in a vibrant sauce of Chinkiang vinegar, sugar, and ketchup with colourful peppers and pineapple. The sauce should glaze the pork thinly — not drown it — maintaining the crispiness of the coating.

steps.instruction: Marinate pork with soy sauce for 10 minutes. Beat the egg with cornflour to make a batter. Coat the pork.
Deep-fry at 170°C for 5-6 minutes until golden. Drain. Re-fry at 190°C for 1-2 minutes until very crispy.
In a clean wok, mix ketchup, vinegar, sugar, and 2 tbsp water. Bring to a simmer. Add peppers, onion, and pineapple. Stir-fry 1 minute.
Add the crispy pork and toss quickly to glaze. Serve immediately.

image_placeholder: Crispy golden sweet and sour pork with colourful peppers and pineapple in a glossy sauce.
wine_pairing_notes: A cold Tsingtao beer or an off-dry Riesling.
tips: Double-fry for extra crispiness — first fry cooks, second fry crisps.
Toss in the sauce quickly and serve immediately — the coating softens fast.
Chinkiang vinegar gives a more complex, mellow sourness than rice vinegar.

title: Yue Bing
description: Moon cakes are the traditional pastry of the Mid-Autumn Festival, featuring a tender, golden pastry shell filled with sweet lotus seed paste and salted duck egg yolk. This simplified version uses a more accessible pastry dough and store-bought lotus paste. The iconic round shape symbolises reunion and completeness. Traditionally stamped with decorative designs before baking.

steps.instruction: Mix golden syrup, oil, and bicarb water. Add flour and mix to form a soft dough. Rest for 2 hours.
If using salted egg yolks, bake them at 180°C for 5 minutes. Divide lotus paste into 8 portions. Wrap each around a yolk (if using) to form balls.
Divide dough into 8 pieces. Flatten each and wrap around a lotus paste ball, sealing completely. Press into a moon cake mould or shape by hand into round, flat cakes.
Brush with beaten egg yolk. Bake at 180°C for 10 minutes, rotate, brush again, and bake 10 more minutes until golden.
Cool completely. Store in an airtight container for 1-2 days before eating — this is called 'returning oil' (hui you) and allows the pastry to soften and the flavours to meld.

image_placeholder: Golden moon cakes with decorative patterns on a plate, one cut open to show the lotus paste and egg yolk filling.
wine_pairing_notes: Chinese pu-erh or oolong tea.
tips: Wait 1-2 days after baking before eating — the pastry softens and improves dramatically.
Golden syrup is essential for the characteristic tender, golden pastry.
A moon cake mould gives the authentic stamped pattern — they are inexpensive and widely available online.

title: Phở Bò
description: Phở bò is Vietnam's national dish — a deeply aromatic beef noodle soup with a clear, star anise and cinnamon-scented broth, flat rice noodles, and thinly sliced beef. The broth is everything: beef bones are charred and simmered for hours with toasted spices, charred onion, and ginger. Served with a platter of fresh herbs, bean sprouts, lime, and chillies for each diner to customise.

ingredients.name: beef sirloin
ingredients.notes: very thinly sliced, for raw topping

steps.instruction: Blanch bones in boiling water for 10 minutes. Drain and rinse. Char onion and ginger under a grill until blackened. Toast spices in a dry pan.

In a large pot, combine bones, brisket, charred aromatics, spices (in a muslin bag), fish sauce, sugar, and 3 litres water. Bring to a boil, skim, and simmer gently for 3 hours. Remove brisket after 2 hours when tender, slice thinly.
Strain the broth. Season with more fish sauce and salt to taste. The broth should be clear and deeply flavourful.

Cook rice noodles. Divide between bowls. Top with sliced brisket and raw sirloin slices. Ladle boiling broth over (the hot broth cooks the raw beef). Serve with bean sprouts, Thai basil, lime, chilli, and hoisin sauce.

image_placeholder: A steaming bowl of phở bò with rice noodles, sliced beef, and fresh herbs, with a plate of bean sprouts, lime, and basil alongside.
tips: Charring the onion and ginger is essential — it gives the broth its characteristic flavour.
Skim the broth regularly for a clear, clean result.
The raw sirloin cooks in the hot broth — slice it paper-thin.

title: Bánh Mì
description: Bánh mì is Vietnam's iconic street food sandwich — a legacy of French colonialism fused with Vietnamese flavours. A light, airy baguette (crispier and airier than French ones) is spread with pâté and mayonnaise, then filled with a protein (often char siu pork, Vietnamese ham, or grilled pork), pickled carrots and daikon, fresh coriander, cucumber, and sliced chillies. The combination of textures and flavours in each bite — crispy, creamy, tangy, fresh, spicy — is legendary.

ingredients.name: char siu pork or grilled pork
carrots
ingredients.notes: julienned, quick-pickled in vinegar and sugar

substitutions.notes: make sure it is light and airy

steps.instruction: Quick-pickle the carrots and daikon: toss with rice vinegar, sugar, and a pinch of salt. Let sit at least 30 minutes.
Warm the baguettes in the oven at 180°C for 3-4 minutes until crispy on the outside.
Split each baguette lengthwise. Spread one side with pâté and the other with mayonnaise. Layer the pork, cucumber, pickled vegetables, coriander, and chilli. Sprinkle with Maggi seasoning if using.

image_placeholder: A Vietnamese bánh mì overflowing with pork, pickled vegetables, coriander, and chilli slices.
tips: The baguette must be light and airy — a dense baguette ruins the experience.
The pickled vegetables are essential — they provide the contrast that makes bánh mì special.
Pâté and mayonnaise together are traditional and non-negotiable.

title: Gỏi Cuốn
description: Gỏi cuốn are Vietnam's fresh, unfried spring rolls — translucent rice paper wrappers filled with prawns, rice vermicelli, herbs, lettuce, and sometimes pork, all visible through the delicate wrapper. Served with a peanut hoisin dipping sauce, they are light, refreshing, and healthy. The wrapping technique requires a gentle touch — the rice paper must be just softened, not mushy.

steps.instruction: Make the dipping sauce: mix hoisin, peanut butter, chilli sauce, and warm water until smooth. Set aside.
Briefly dip a rice paper wrapper in warm water for 5-10 seconds until just pliable (it will continue softening). Lay flat on a damp tea towel.

Layer lettuce, vermicelli, herbs, and carrot on the lower third. Fold the bottom up over the filling, fold in the sides, then place prawns along the fold line (cut side facing out so they show through). Roll tightly to close.
Repeat with remaining wrappers. Serve immediately with the dipping sauce.

image_placeholder: Fresh summer rolls on a plate showing the prawns and herbs visible through the translucent wrapper, with a peanut dipping sauce.
wine_pairing_notes: A cold Vietnamese beer (333 or Saigon) or a dry Riesling.
tips: Do not over-soak the rice paper — it should be pliable but still firm; it continues to soften.
Place the prawns cut-side against the wrapper so they are visible from outside.
Serve immediately — the wrappers dry out and become tough if left too long.

title: Bún Chả
description: Bún chả is Hanoi's signature dish — charcoal-grilled pork patties and caramelised pork belly slices served in a bowl of sweet-sour-salty dipping broth (nước chấm) alongside rice vermicelli, fresh herbs, and pickled vegetables. Each diner dips noodles and herbs into the broth with the grilled pork. The dish gained international fame when Barack Obama ate it with Anthony Bourdain at a Hanoi street stall.

steps.instruction: Marinate the sliced pork and form the minced pork into small flat patties. Marinate both in fish sauce, 1 tbsp sugar, garlic, shallot, and pepper for at least 1 hour.
Make the dipping broth: dissolve remaining sugar in warm water. Add fish sauce, lime juice, rice vinegar, sliced garlic, and chilli. It should be a balance of sweet, sour, salty, and spicy.
Grill the pork slices and patties over high heat (charcoal preferred) for 2-3 minutes per side until charred and caramelised.
Divide the grilled pork into bowls of the dipping broth. Serve alongside rice vermicelli, fresh herbs, and pickled vegetables. Dip everything into the broth to eat.

image_placeholder: Bowls of bún chả with charcoal-grilled pork patties and slices in broth, alongside rice vermicelli, fresh herbs, and pickled vegetables.
wine_pairing_notes: A cold Vietnamese beer (Bia Hơi or 333).
tips: Charcoal grilling is what makes bún chả special — a grill pan is an acceptable substitute.
The dipping broth must balance all four flavours: sweet, sour, salty, spicy.
Bún chả is an assembly dish — serve all components separately for diners to combine.

title: Cơm Tấm
description: Cơm tấm is Saigon's beloved everyday dish — broken rice grains served with grilled marinated pork chops (sườn nướng), a fried egg, shredded pork skin, pickled vegetables, and a drizzle of sweet fish sauce (nước mắm). The broken rice was historically a cheaper byproduct of rice milling, adopted by the working class and now elevated to a city-wide obsession.

steps.instruction: Marinate pork chops with lemongrass, garlic, shallot, fish sauce, sugar, and oil for at least 2 hours.
Cook the broken rice according to package instructions (similar to regular rice but slightly more water).
Grill or pan-fry the pork chops over high heat for 4-5 minutes per side until charred and cooked through. Fry the eggs.
Plate: rice on one side, pork chop alongside, topped with a fried egg. Add pickled vegetables, cucumber, and tomato. Drizzle sweet fish sauce over everything.

image_placeholder: A plate of broken rice with a charred pork chop, fried egg, pickled vegetables, and fresh garnishes.
wine_pairing_notes: A cold Vietnamese beer.
tips: Lemongrass and garlic in the marinade are what give the pork its distinctive flavour.
Broken rice has a unique texture — slightly stickier and softer than regular rice.
Sweet fish sauce (nước mắm pha) for drizzling: mix fish sauce, sugar, lime juice, garlic, and chilli.

title: Bánh Xèo
description: Bánh xèo — named for the sizzling sound (xèo!) when the batter hits the hot pan — is a crispy Vietnamese crepe made from rice flour and turmeric batter, filled with prawns, pork belly, bean sprouts, and spring onion. Eaten by wrapping pieces in lettuce and herbs and dipping in nước chấm. The crepe should be lacy and shatteringly crispy.

ingredients.name: nước chấm
ingredients.notes: dipping sauce (fish sauce, lime, sugar, garlic, chilli)

steps.instruction: Mix rice flour, cornflour, turmeric, and salt. Whisk in coconut milk and water until smooth. Add spring onions. Rest batter 30 minutes.
Heat oil in a non-stick pan over high heat. Add a few slices of pork and prawns. Cook 1 minute.
Pour a thin layer of batter over, swirling to coat. Add bean sprouts on one half. Cover and cook 2 minutes until the edges are crispy and lacy.
Fold in half. Slide onto a plate. Repeat with remaining batter. Serve with lettuce, herbs, and nước chấm for wrapping and dipping.

image_placeholder: A crispy golden bánh xèo folded in half on a plate, with lettuce, herbs, and dipping sauce alongside.
wine_pairing_notes: A cold Vietnamese beer.
tips: The pan must be very hot when the batter is added — this creates the sizzle and crispiness.
Use a thin layer of batter for the crispiest result.
Wrapping in lettuce and herbs before dipping is the traditional way to eat it.

title: Phở Gà
description: Phở gà is the lighter, more delicate counterpart to beef pho, featuring a clear, fragrant chicken broth. The broth is quicker to make than beef pho but no less flavourful, with the same charred ginger and onion, star anise, and coriander seeds. Topped with poached chicken, fresh herbs, and a squeeze of lime.

ingredients.name: whole chicken
ingredients.notes: about 1.5kg, or chicken carcass plus thighs

steps.instruction: Place chicken in a large pot with 2.5 litres cold water. Bring to a boil, skim thoroughly. Add charred onion and ginger, spices, fish sauce, and sugar. Simmer gently for 1 hour.
Remove the chicken. Shred the meat, discard bones. Strain and season the broth.
Cook rice noodles. Divide between bowls. Top with shredded chicken, ladle hot broth over. Serve with herbs, bean sprouts, and lime.

image_placeholder: A bowl of phở gà with rice noodles, shredded chicken, and fresh herbs in a clear golden broth.
tips: Chicken pho is quicker than beef — 1 hour for the broth instead of 3+.
Gentle simmering keeps the broth clear — vigorous boiling makes it cloudy.
The fresh herbs are essential — do not skip them.

title: Canh Chua
description: Canh chua is Vietnam's vibrant sour soup from the Mekong Delta — a tangy, sweet, and savoury broth made with tamarind, tomatoes, pineapple, and catfish or prawns. Bean sprouts, okra, and fresh herbs are added at the end. The sourness from tamarind is balanced by sweetness from pineapple, creating a refreshing, complex soup that is a staple of Southern Vietnamese home cooking.

steps.instruction: Bring water to a boil. Add tamarind water, fish sauce, sugar, and tomatoes. Simmer 10 minutes.
Add pineapple, okra, and fish/prawns. Cook 5-7 minutes until the fish is just cooked.
Add bean sprouts. Cook 1 minute. Remove from heat and add fresh herbs and chilli. Serve with steamed rice.

image_placeholder: A bowl of canh chua sour soup with fish, pineapple, tomatoes, and bean sprouts in a tangy broth, garnished with herbs.
wine_pairing_notes: A cold Vietnamese beer.
tips: The tamarind provides the sourness — the balance with sugar and fish sauce is key.
Add the herbs off the heat to preserve their freshness.
Traditionally served with steamed rice, not eaten as a standalone soup.

title: Bún Bò Huế
title_en: Spicy Beef Noodle Soup from Hue
description: Bún bò Huế is the fiery noodle soup of Hue — Vietnam's former imperial capital. The broth is more robust and spicy than phở, featuring lemongrass, shrimp paste, chilli oil, and both beef and pork. The thick, round rice noodles (bún bò) have a distinctive chewiness. Topped with sliced beef shank, pork knuckle, and Vietnamese pork sausage, it is a bold, complex bowl.

ingredients.name: fresh herbs
ingredients.notes: mint, coriander, banana blossom, shredded cabbage

steps.instruction: Blanch bones and pork hock. Start fresh with 3 litres water, bones, pork hock, and beef shank. Simmer 2 hours, skimming regularly. Remove pork hock after 1 hour, beef shank after 1.5 hours. Slice when cool.
In a small pan, heat annatto/chilli oil. Fry shrimp paste and chopped lemongrass for 2 minutes. Add to the broth with fish sauce and sugar. Simmer 30 more minutes.
Strain the broth. Cook the noodles. Assemble bowls: noodles, sliced meats, hot broth. Serve with herbs, lime, and chilli sauce.

image_placeholder: A bowl of bún bò Huế with thick round noodles, sliced beef, pork hock, in a red-orange spicy broth.
tips: Shrimp paste (mắm ruốc) gives the broth its distinctive funky depth — do not skip it.
The broth should be spicier and more robust than phở.
The thick round noodles are essential for the authentic texture.

title: Cá Kho Tộ
description: Cá kho tộ is a quintessential Vietnamese home-cooked dish — fish steaks (traditionally catfish or other firm fish) braised in a caramel sauce of coconut water, fish sauce, and cracked black pepper in a small clay pot. The caramel sauce is deeply savoury-sweet and becomes thick and sticky as it reduces. The dish is one of the most beloved everyday meals in Vietnam, eaten with steamed rice and a light vegetable soup.

substitutions.substitute: firm white fish like cod or halibut
substitutions.notes: needs to be firm enough not to break apart

steps.instruction: Make the caramel: heat sugar in a small clay pot or saucepan over medium heat until it melts and turns deep amber. Add a splash of fish sauce (it will sizzle) and coconut water. Stir until the caramel dissolves.
Add the shallots, garlic, remaining fish sauce, and pepper. Nestle the fish steaks in the sauce. Bring to a simmer.
Cover and braise on low heat for 20-25 minutes, spooning the sauce over the fish occasionally. The sauce should reduce to a thick, sticky glaze.
Garnish with spring onion, chilli, and cracked black pepper. Serve with steamed rice.

image_placeholder: A clay pot of cá kho tộ with fish steaks in a dark caramel sauce, garnished with cracked pepper and spring onion.
wine_pairing_notes: A cold Vietnamese beer.
tips: Make the caramel dark amber — too light and it tastes simply sweet; too dark and it is bitter.
Coconut water (not coconut milk) is the traditional liquid — it adds a subtle sweetness.
The clay pot retains heat and adds to the flavour — a heavy saucepan is the alternative.

title: Chè Ba Màu
description: Chè ba màu is Vietnam's vibrant three-colour dessert — a layered parfait of red beans (đậu đỏ), green mung bean paste, and yellow mung bean paste, topped with coconut milk and crushed ice. The three layers create a beautiful traffic-light effect in the glass. It is sweet, creamy, and refreshing — the perfect end to a spicy Vietnamese meal on a hot day. Each layer has its own distinct flavour and texture.

steps.instruction: Cook the mung beans: soak for 2 hours, then simmer until soft. Divide in half. For the yellow layer, mash with 30g sugar. For the green layer, mash with 30g sugar and pandan extract or boiled pandan leaves.
Simmer the red beans with 30g sugar for 10 minutes until sweet and soft. Let cool.
Warm the coconut milk with remaining sugar and a pinch of salt. Let cool.
Layer in tall glasses: red beans at the bottom, yellow mung bean in the middle, green mung bean on top. Top with crushed ice and pour coconut milk over.

image_placeholder: Tall glasses of chè ba màu showing three distinct coloured layers — red, yellow, green — topped with coconut milk and crushed ice.
tips: The layers should be distinct — let each cool before layering.
Pandan gives the green layer its colour and a distinctive floral aroma.
Adjust sweetness to your taste — this should be refreshing, not cloying.

title: Bò Lá Lốt
description: Bò lá lốt are fragrant parcels of spiced minced beef wrapped in betel leaves (lá lốt) and grilled or pan-fried until the leaves are charred and the filling is cooked through. The betel leaves impart a unique peppery, slightly anise-like flavour that is unmistakable in Vietnamese cuisine. These are a popular street food snack, often served with rice noodles, fresh herbs, and nước chấm.

steps.instruction: Mix the minced beef with lemongrass, garlic, shallot, fish sauce, sugar, five-spice, and pepper. Let marinate 30 minutes.
Place a tablespoon of filling at the base of each betel leaf. Roll up tightly, tucking in the sides. Secure with a toothpick if needed.
Pan-fry in oil over medium-high heat for 2-3 minutes per side until the leaves are charred and the meat is cooked through. Or grill over charcoal.
Serve with rice noodles, fresh herbs, pickled vegetables, and nước chấm dipping sauce.

image_placeholder: Charred bò lá lốt rolls on a plate with fresh herbs, rice noodles, and a small dish of nước chấm dipping sauce.
wine_pairing_notes: A cold Vietnamese beer.
tips: Betel leaves (lá lốt) are available at Asian grocers — they have a unique flavour that is hard to replicate.
If unavailable, perilla or shiso leaves are the best substitute for a similar aromatic quality.
Charcoal grilling gives the best smoky flavour, but pan-frying is quicker.
